title: AI in Life Sciences: What's Changing and What's Holding Us Back

yaml.title: AI in Life Sciences: What's Changing and What's Holding Us Back

markdown.markdown:
This log summarizes key insights from the webinar *Inside the Minds of Scientists: How AI is Changing Life Science Research*, hosted by **Bioinformatics** in May 2025.  
🎥 [Watch the webinar on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1086878814)  
📊 [Download the slide deck (PDF)](https://24148121.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hubfs/24148121/BioInformatics/Webinar%20Decks/BioInfo-How-AI-Is-Changing-Life-Science-Research-Webinar-05222025.pdf)



## 🚀 Key Trends

- AI is moving from discovery to development.
- AI appears to be shifting from predictions to decisions — Y Combinator is investing heavily in companies fully run by AI agents.
- Pharma is integrating AI across more stages of R&D (e.g., target ID, trial design, decision-making).

## 🧱 What's Holding AI Back in Biology?

> Current issues with Ai in biosciences:

- **Reproducibility issues** — hard to trust models without transparent workflows.
- **Learning curve and training** — lack of experience with AI was a significant pain point for many users.


💬 *This resonated with me. I’ve already seen how messy biological data can be, which leads to a steep learning curve and difficulty managing AI outputs.*

## 📘 Changes for future adoption

- Transparency is important for scientists to adopt and understand AI tools — they need to know how and what models were trained with.
- Tools need to be built with their actual users in mind — typically, biologists.

## 🧠 About

This log summarizes key insights from the webinar *Inside the Minds of Scientists: How AI is Changing Life Science Research*, hosted by **Bioinformatics** in May 2025.  
🎥 [Watch the webinar on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1086878814)  
📊 [Download the slide deck (PDF)](https://24148121.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hubfs/24148121/BioInformatics/Webinar%20Decks/BioInfo-How-AI-Is-Changing-Life-Science-Research-Webinar-05222025.pdf)



## 🚀 Key Trends

- AI is moving from discovery to development.
- AI appears to be shifting from predictions to decisions — Y Combinator is investing heavily in companies fully run by AI agents.
- Pharma is integrating AI across more stages of R&D (e.g., target ID, trial design, decision-making).

## 🧱 What's Holding AI Back in Biology?

> Current issues with Ai in biosciences:

- **Reproducibility issues** — hard to trust models without transparent workflows.
- **Learning curve and training** — lack of experience with AI was a significant pain point for many users.


💬 *This resonated with me. I’ve already seen how messy biological data can be, which leads to a steep learning curve and difficulty managing AI outputs.*

## 📘 Changes for future adoption

- Transparency is important for scientists to adopt and understand AI tools — they need to know how and what models were trained with.
- Tools need to be built with their actual users in mind — typically, biologists.
